REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J

On 20 December 2024 another judge of this court made consent property adjustment orders between the parties. By his further amended Application – Enforcement filed on 26 February 2026 the husband, who was the respondent in the property adjustment proceedings seeks orders that he says are by way of enforcement of some of those consent orders. By her Application in a Proceeding filed on 27 August 2025 the wife, who was the applicant in the property adjustment proceedings, opposes the orders sought by the husband and seeks orders that she says are by way enforcement of certain aspects of the consent orders.

The orders

Between them, the parties had and continue to have extensive property interests held by each of them in a web of companies and trusts. In overview, however, the consent orders provided for:

(a) the wife to:

(i) retain outright her cash, bank accounts, personal property, motor vehicle and partial settlement already received;

(ii) receive two residential properties situate in O Street, Suburb P free of encumbrance;

(iii) receive all shares in E Pty Ltd and become its sole director, with exclusive control of its bank accounts;

(iv) receive 65% of the net proceeds of sale of certain other real estate described as M Street Property upon sale;

(v) receive 100% of the husband’s interest in Self-Managed Super Fund 1, subject to a superannuation split back to the husband so that he retains $100,000; and

(vi) be released from all involvement, risk and future liability in the Michelaki Group, including guarantees, tax exposure and management roles.

(b) the husband to:

(i) retain his cash, bank accounts, personal property, Chinese real property and partial settlement already received;

(ii) retain the balance of the Michelaki Group, including control of trusts and companies not specifically transferred;

(iii) receive 35% of the net proceeds of sale of the M Street Property;

(iv) retain his Self-Managed Super Fund 2, into which his remaining Self-Managed Super Fund 1 balance is to be rolled over; and

(v) be indemnified by the wife in relation to E Pty Ltd and Self-Managed Super Fund 1, except for fraud or negligence.

Relevant to the present applications are the orders dealing with:

(a) the disposition of the husband’s rights, title and interest in two residential properties situated at O Street, Suburb P, Queensland (orders 9, 10, 11 and 12);

(b) the disposition of certain residential properties situated at M Street, Suburb N, Queensland (orders 13 and 14);

(c) the disposition of the husband’s interests in E Pty Ltd (orders 17-20); and

(d) the disposition of the husband’s interest in Self-Managed Super Fund 1 (orders 53-67).

S Pty Ltd , as trustee of the Q Property Trust, is the registered proprietor of the O Street properties. S Pty Ltd is controlled by the husband. Pursuant to orders 9, 10 and 12 of the consent orders, the husband was to cause S Pty Ltd to transfer the O Street properties to the wife, free of encumbrance, on or before 20 March 2025. He was to assume liability for and indemnify the applicant against all outgoings for the properties pending their transfer.

The phrase “[M Street] Property” in the orders, refers collectively to residential Property 1,2,3,4 at M Street, Suburb N and Property 5 at M Street, Suburb N. The registered proprietor of M Street Property was K2 Pty Ltd. The husband is the sole director of K2 Pty Ltd. The sole shareholder of K2 Pty Ltd is F2 Pty Ltd, the trustee of the Michelaki Family Trust, of which both parties are beneficiaries. The trustee is controlled by the husband.

Pursuant to order 13, the husband was to cause K2 Pty Ltd to sell the M Street Property and distribute 65% of the proceeds to the wife and 35% to himself, nett of certain expenses. The properties have now all been sold.

The husband is the sole director of E Pty Ltd. F Pty Ltd, another company controlled by the husband, holds 50 ordinary shares in E Pty Ltd. The remaining shares are held by Mr R (20 per cent), Mr T (20 per cent) and Mr U (10 per cent). Pursuant to orders 17 and 18 of the consent orders, the husband is to cause F Pty Ltd to transfer its shareholding in E Pty Ltd to the wife and thereafter he must resign as a director of E Pty Ltd. The wife is to be appointed in his stead.

The trustee of Self-Managed Super Fund 1 is V Pty Ltd. Both parties are members. The consent orders seek to achieve a number of things in relation to this fund, namely:

(a) a split of 100% of the husband’s interests to the wife;

(b) a split of the wife’s interest to the husband so that as at the operative date, the husband’s interest was $100,000;

(c) the rolling out of the husband’s final interest in the fund to Self-Managed Super Fund 2, another fund of which he is a member;

(d) the removal of the husband as a director and member of V Pty Ltd; and

(e) the removal of the husband as a member of the fund.

The settlement date for the parties’ obligations generally under the consent orders was 20 March 2025.

The husband’s enforcement application

The husband claims to seek enforcement of orders 9-12, 13(q) and 17-20 of the consent orders. He seeks eleven orders by way of enforcement.

At the outset I record the wife’s submission that the orders sought by the husband in his application are not and cannot be said to be enforcement orders. She argues that having filed an Application – Enforcement, none of the orders he seeks in it relate to an enforcement obligation or order defined in r 11.01 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth).

I accept that argument. The orders sought by the husband do not fit comfortably with the enforcement regime set out in Part 11.1 of the FCFCR. Rather, his application is better conceptualised as a claim for orders relating variously to the implementation and interpretation of the consent orders. Arguably, some might be said to be consequential orders following upon disputes between the parties about the implementation and interpretation of the consent orders. The parties agreed that each would be able to bring applications for such orders upon notice to the other (see order 78 of the consent orders).

Notwithstanding its form, I have determined to treat the husband’s application and the wife’s response to it as applications authorised by order 78 of the consent orders. That is not to say that the individual orders each seeks can be characterised as such. I have dealt with each claimed order below. Neither party can be said to be prejudiced by such an approach. The parties have been on notice of the applications for a considerable period and each have filed outlines of argument that deal with the substantive issues thrown up by their positions.

The O Street properties

The first order the husband seeks is the “[r]elease the rental income of [Property 1 and 2] at [O Street] to [Q Property Trust], the owner of the properties, from March 2025 to end of January 2026”. “[Property 1 and 2 at O Street] ” is, by reason of the definitions in the consent orders, a reference to the O Street properties referred to in order 9.

The two properties were encumbered by registered mortgages held in favour of the National Australia Bank. The transfer of the O Street properties to the wife was to occur on or before the Settlement Date. For reasons that are not now relevant, the husband could not achieve a transfer of the O Street properties to the wife free of encumbrance on or before the settlement date.

However, he was able to secure the relevant discharges on 7 August 2025. He immediately told the wife that he could transfer the properties to her and called upon her to furnish the necessary transfer documents for him to sign. However, despite the obligation upon her to produce the documents necessary to effect the transfer, she did not do so until late 2025.

Oddly, in response to the husband’s enforcement application and notwithstanding a provision in the consent orders to identical effect (see order 68 thereof) the wife sought relief pursuant to s 106A of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) so that a registrar could sign the necessary documents to effect the transfer to her. Since the filing of these competing applications, however, the parties have been able to resolve the issue about the transfer of these properties. The precise date of transfer is not clear on the evidence, but it seems that it occurred in early February 2026. There is no longer a dispute about the transfer of the O Street properties. The 106B relief is now unnecessary and was abandoned in the wife’s Further Amended Application in a Proceeding filed on 27 February 2026.

Both before and after the consent orders were made, the O Street properties were let and produced an income. On 31 March 2025, the wife caused her solicitors to write to the property manager of the O Street properties (the parties’ daughter) demanding that she withhold the rental payments for the properties from S Pty Ltd “until the issue is resolved and the properties are transferred to [Ms Michelaki].” The wife deposes that the property manager has since transferred the withheld rent to her, rather than S Pty Ltd. It is uncontroversial that since the wife’s solicitor’s letter to the managing agent, S Pty Ltd has not received any rent in respect of the properties.

The husband caused S Pty Ltd to pay the outgoings for the O Street properties up to May 2025 and thereafter the wife has met the outgoings for those properties.

Until the wife filed her Further Amended Application in a Proceeding on 27 February 2026 the remaining dispute concerned the parties’ entitlement to the income from the properties and the liability for their outgoings between the date of the consent orders and their transfer to the husband or perhaps May 2025. By her amended process the wife claims that the husband should pay to her $1,336.18 being the total for three water and sewage invoices that fell due on 9 May 2025, 7 August 2025 and 13 August 2025 in respect of the properties. However, she abandoned this claim at the hearing before me.

What remains in dispute concerning the O Street properties then, is the husband’s claim in his Further Amended Application – Enforcement filed on 26 February 2026 that the wife pay the rent received in respect of the O Street properties from March 2025 to the end of January 2026 to S Pty Ltd. That is so notwithstanding that the wife has from at least August 2025 and perhaps since as early as May 2025, paid the outgoings for the properties. There is no evidence about the precise amount of rental income that was derived from the O Street properties, nor the outgoings and expenses from the time of the consent orders until May 2025 or the date of transfer.

Before the consent orders, S Pty Ltd was entitled to receive the rent from the O Street properties as it was the registered proprietor of the relevant properties. Between the making of the orders and the transfer of them to the wife, the wife was the equitable owner of the properties (Official Trustee in Bankruptcy v Mateo [2003] FCAFC 26; (2003) 127 FCR 217 at [57], [102], [128]). She remained so until her equitable and legal interests merged upon her becoming the registered proprietor of the O Street properties.

However, that did not mean that she became entitled to receive the rents and profits of the O Street properties from the unobserved settlement date. As Deane J explained in Kern Corporation Ltd v Walter Reid Trading Pty Ltd [1987] HCA 20; (1987) 163 CLR 164, at 191-192:

True it is that, pending payment of the purchase price, the purchaser has an equitable interest in the land which reflects the extent to which equitable remedies are available to protect his contractual rights and that the vendor is under obligations in equity which attach to the land. Nonetheless, the vendor himself retains a continuing beneficial estate in the land which transcends any "lien" for unpaid purchase money to which he may be entitled in equity after completion. Pending completion, he is beneficially entitled to possession and use. Pending completion, he is beneficially entitled to the rents and profits. If the purchaser enters upon the land without the vendor's permission and without authority under the contract, the vendor can maintain, for his own benefit, an action for trespass against the purchaser. While the practical significance of those continuing beneficial rights of the vendor may vary according to particular circumstances (e.g. whether completion is already overdue or is not due for some lengthy period), it is both inaccurate and misleading to speak of the unpaid vendor under an uncompleted contract as a trustee for the purchaser (see, generally, per Brett L.J., Rayner v. Preston (1881) 18 ChD 1, at pp 10-11 and Waters, The Constructive Trust, (1964), pp 74ff.). There is authority for the view that, after completion has actually taken place, some of the equitable rights of the purchaser, which (in their entirety) then constitute beneficial ownership, relate back to the date of the contract. But there is no relation back of beneficial ownership in the sense that the vendor is retrospectively deprived of his beneficial right, pending payment of the full purchase price, to the possession, use, rents and profits of the land. Regardless of whether his rights be viewed in the perspective of foresight (i.e. before completion) or hindsight (i.e. after completion), the ordinary unpaid vendor of land is not a trustee of the land for the purchaser. Nor is it accurate to refer to such a vendor as a "trustee sub modo" unless the disarming mystique of the added Latin is treated as a warrant for essential misdescription.

In the absence of an express provision to the contrary in the consent orders, I am of the view that S Pty Ltd remained entitled to receive the rents and profits from the O Street properties until legal title to them was transferred to the wife. Her action in directing the managing agent to withhold those rents from the company and subsequently to pay them to her was wrongful. Without more, S Pty Ltd could maintain an action against the wife for money had and received in respect of the rents and profits received by her in respect of the O Street properties until the transfer of title to her.

Similarly, the husband “in his capacity as director of [S Pty Ltd]” would remain liable for all outgoings “in and about these properties” as provided for in order 11 of the consent orders.

Counsel for the wife’s concession that S Pty Ltd would be entitled to the rents and profits of the O Street properties rather than the wife was well made. The wife is liable to disgorge to the company (not the husband) those rents. However, she has also made payments on behalf of the company from, it seems, August 2025. She would be entitled to recall those payments from the company as money paid to the use of the company, or money paid under a mistake of fact or law (it matters not which).

It is clear then that an accounting of sorts is required to determine where the balance of those accounts might fall. But the evidence is entirely inadequate to enable the necessary reconciliation to be undertaken. The husband asserts that the amount of rent to be repaid is $45,000, but how that is calculated is not at all apparent from the evidence. I cannot be satisfied on the balance of probabilities or otherwise as to the amount that might be owed to the husband “as director of [S Pty Ltd]” or to the wife for that matter on her claim for the reimbursement of outgoing for the properties (noting her abandonment of this claim).

In those circumstances, the husband’s claim for relief in respect of the funds received by the wife as the rents of the O Street properties before their transfer to her is dismissed.

Order 2 of the Application – Enforcement

Pursuant to the final consent orders, the husband was to cause K2 Pty Ltd to sell the M Street Property. It is uncontroversial that the properties have all been sold and the proceeds of sale are held on trust by the wife’s solicitors on behalf of the parties. As at the hearing before me, the amount held on trust (apparently across 2 accounts) was $4,404,462.25.

The orders sought by the husband about the distribution of these proceeds overlaps in some respects with the relief sought by the wife. I have dealt with those common areas here and I have dealt with the claims made by the wife that might be said to be independent of the husband’s claims later in these reasons.

Order 13(q) of the consent orders provides:

(q) the proceeds of sale of the [M Street Property] shall be paid in the following manner and priority:

(1) any encumbrance affecting the properties including rates, taxes and other reasonable expenses;

(2) the real estate agent’s commission and any required advertising or marketing expenses;

(3) the legal costs associated with the sale of the [M Street Property];

(4) payment of any income tax for [K2 Pty Ltd] arising from the capital gain from the sale of each [M Street Property]. Such amount to be certified by the accountant for [K2 Pty Ltd] and if disputed by the wife, as certified by [Ms W] of [X Company];

(5) reimbursement to the husband for any payments made by him pursuant to paragraph 13(b) [ie repair costs] and/or 13(i) [ie costs of advertising the sales] of this Order;

(6) payment of 2/3 of the tax payable by [K Discretionary Trus]t and the [Y Family Trust] arising from debt forgiveness provisions at paragraphs 24 to 27 of this Order; and

(7) the balance to be paid and distributed to the parties in the following proportions:

(A) 65% to the wife’s nominated bank account; and

(B) 35% to the husband’s nominated bank account.

The deductions to be made from the gross proceeds of sale of the M Street Property pursuant to order 13(q)(1) are subject to three disputes between the parties.

The first concerns an amount of goods and services tax that the husband argues is a legitimate deduction from the gross sale proceeds of the M Street Property pursuant to order 13(q)(1). There appears to be no dispute that the sale of two of the properties (Property 2 and Property 5) gives rise to $37,610.14 by way of a goods and services tax liability on the part of K2 Pty Ltd. The wife opposes this amount being deducted from the gross sale proceeds pursuant to order 13(q)(1).

The husband’s construction of order 13(q)(1) is erroneous. Order 13(q)(1) does not extend to capture goods and services tax as he contends. I accept the wife’s argument that order 13(q)(1) contemplates encumbrances affecting the properties, including taxes. Goods and services tax is not such a liability. It is not an encumbrance upon the title to any of the lots comprising M Street Property. As the wife points out in her submissions, some taxes and charges are encumbrances on the title to property or run with the land and need to be cleared at the time of sale to pass an unimpeachable title to a purchaser. Examples include land tax pursuant to the Land Tax Act 2010 (Qld) and overdue rates and charges pursuant to the Local Government Act 2009 (Qld) and, locally, the City of Brisbane Act 2010 (Qld). Goods and services tax imposed by the New Tax System (Goods and Services Tax) Act 1999 (Cth) is not a charge or encumbrance affecting the lots comprising the M Street Property. It is not an expense caught by order 13(q)(1) of the consent orders. The GST ought to be paid separately by K2 Pty Ltd, not from the proceeds of sale.

The second dispute concerns $3,047.00 in accountancy fees which the husband says ought to be deducted from the sale proceeds. These fees include a $750 CGT/GST calculation fee for the properties; a $600 “Service Fee - CGT/GST / Distribution calcualtion to provide to laywer (sic)” fee; a $198 BAS Statement fee; and a $1,499.00 2024 Financial Statement and Company Tax Return fee issued by Z Accounting Services to K2 Pty Ltd.

The husband contends that these fees are reasonable expenses for the purposes of order 13(1q)(1) and accordingly ought to be taken up under that order. However, whilst the expenses might have been reasonably incurred by K2 Pty Ltd for the purposes of its business, the only aspect of which might have been the development and sale of the M Street Property, they are not reasonable expenses associated with the sale of the M Street Property. In my view, they are not within the phrase reasonable expenses as used in order 13(q)(1) of the consent orders because that order contemplates the reasonable expenses associated with the sale of the properties, not the reasonable expenses of K2 Pty Ltd attending to its taxation and other obligations consequent upon the sale of the properties. Whilst I do not consider that reasonable expenses for the purposes of this order need to be encumbrances on the title to the property or the subject of charges over the property in the same way that taxes and charges do, the expenses claimed must be referable to the sale of the property or the individual lots, not referable to the business affairs of the company.

I reject the husband’s claim that these amounts should be taken up under order 13(q)(1).

The third dispute concerns the husband’s claim that $12,519.99, representing “rates and other reasonable expenses” from the sales after 20 December 2024 should be deducted from the sale proceeds. Part of this amount relates to council rates and part relates to further accounting expenses for K2 Pty Ltd. To the extent that this claim concerns accounting expenses for various work, the claim is rejected for the reason I have just given above. To the extent that it concerns council rates, the relevant expenses were adjusted at the point of sale. If these expenses were to be deducted again from the gross proceeds of sale, they would be double counted. These claims are rejected.

There is no dispute about the quantum or incidence of the deductions provided for in order 13(q)(2) and (3).

Order 1(q)(4) gives rise to two disputes. Both concern the payment of capital gains tax, but they arise in different ways.

As to the first dispute, there is no issue that for the purposes of order 13(q)(4) K2 Pty Ltd has an income tax liability arising from the capital gains from the sale of the M Street Property in a total amount of $856,605.60. The dispute about this amount is about where it should be paid.

The wife says that sum should be paid by her solicitors directly to the Australian Taxation Office. The wife contends that order 13(q)(4) does not, on its proper construction, mean that the income tax assessed on the relevant capital gains should be paid to the husband or K2 Pty Ltd. She argues that the amount should be paid directly to the Tax Office, presumably to K2 Pty Ltd’s credit, so that she can be sure that the company has discharged its tax liability.

However, this argument fails because the orders do not easily accommodate the wife’s interpretation. The orders do not provide for the payment to be made to the Commissioner for Taxation to the company’s credit as they might have. The evidence shows that the company has a tax liability for the agreed sum. No argument is made and no evidence demonstrates that the company will not discharge that liability in due course.

Moreover, the purpose of order 13(q)(4) does not appear to be to ensure that the company discharges its taxation liability. Rather it is to provide the quantum of a deduction from the gross proceeds of sale which, when taken with the other nominated deductions, will derive the nett proceeds of sale that might then be distributed between the parties. It matters not where it is paid, just that the sum to be deducted from the gross proceeds of sale is fixed and taken into account.

The wife argues that she is concerned that the husband may not cause K2 Pty Ltd to pay the liability as it falls due preventing her from receiving potential franking credits from the nett sale proceeds which would be distributed to her through the Michelaki Family Trust. But no evidential basis is identified for her concern. I am entitled to assume that K2 Pty Ltd will meet its obligation to pay assessed taxation.

The relevant amount should be paid to the husband or at his direction. Even though one is not specifically sought, I will make an order to that effect.

The second dispute arising in relation to order 13(q)(4) of the consent orders concerns the bringing to account of a capital gains tax liability of $93,459.60 for K2 Pty Ltd springing from the sale of Property 5 within the M Street Property development. The husband says that this liability ought to be paid from the gross sale proceeds just as the capital gains tax is to be paid in respect of the other properties.

The evidence shows that the parties caused K2 Pty Ltd to enter into a contract of sale for Property 5 on or around 28 July 2023. After accounting for expenses and liabilities, the net proceeds of sale of $513,152.64 were paid to the wife’s solicitors’ trust account. The husband says these funds were $600,000. Whatever the case, those funds were then, by agreement between the parties, disbursed as follows:

(a) $20,235 was used to pay GST on behalf of K2 Pty Ltd arising from the sale of Property 5;

(b) $50,014.60 was used to make repayments of a loan owing by one of the husband’s entities secured against a property site, that he now retains as part of the consent orders;

(c) $210,000 was used to repay Self-Managed Super Fund 1, after the husband withdraw that amount to pay an asserted debt owing to Mr R (more of that later) without the wife’s consent; and

(d) $19,844 was used to pay single expert fees incurred in our substantive family law proceeding;

(e) the balance of $213,059.04 (the husband says $219,512.82) was transferred to the husband pending the finalisation of the property proceedings between the parties.

Save as I have just set out above, the proceeds of sale were not distributed between the parties. The husband received them all after payment of the company’s GST liability and making good his defalcation from the superannuation fund. Moreover, the funds were funds to which K2 Pty Ltd was entitled, not the husband. The husband received sufficient funds to discharge K2 Pty Ltd’s tax liability as a result of the relevant capital gain arising from the sale. This claim cannot succeed.

In addition, there is a further reason to reject this claim. Property 5 is not part of M Street Property for the purposes of the orders (see order 1(v) and the definition of M Street Property). That that is so is not surprising. Property 5 was sold in 2023 well before the consent orders were made. The proceeds of sale were distributed entirely in accordance with the husband’s direction, albeit with the wife’s agreement. He, or his interests, was the beneficiary of the distribution of those funds. His evidence demonstrates that the tax liability arising from this sale has been settled by K2 Pty Ltd.

Were it the case that this amount was to be brought to account in the way in which the husband now contends, a provision in the order to that effect could have been easily added. It could have been accomplished by simply adding Property 5 to the definition of the phrase M Street Property, but that did not happen. The clear inference is that the parties intended that K2 Pty Ltd and or the husband should bear that expense.

The husband’s claim in this regard cannot succeed.

There is no dispute about the incidence or quantum of deductions for the purposes of order 13(q)(5) or (6). There is no dispute about the operation of order 13(q)(7) once the legitimate deductions from the gross proceeds of sale have been identified and made.

Orders 3 of the Application – Enforcement

The husband seeks the wife “[p]ay back $210,000 from [E Pty Ltd] to the [husband] as the shareholder distribution of [E Pty Ltd]”.

This claim has a peripheral connection with order 17 of the consent orders which requires the husband to cause the shareholding held under his control in E Pty Ltd to be transferred to the wife. Order 18 requires him to resign as a director of E Pty Ltd and to cause the wife to be installed as a director in his stead.

E Pty Ltd owned real property at B Street, Suburb D, Qld. The property was sold in November 2024 and the proceeds of sale are held in the wife’s solicitor’s trust account.

According to the husband’s submissions contained within his case outline document:

(a) Mr R was a long-standing friend of the husband;

(b) at times relevant to the present application, Mr R was a 20% shareholder in E Pty Ltd;

(c) during Covid-19 Mr R encountered financial difficulty and he asked the husband’s current wife if she could lend him $170,000 to keep his family business going;

(d) she made a loan to Mr R in October 2022, he says, on condition that Mr R would pay her back immediately when B Street was sold;

(e) the property was under a contract of sale from about 17 August 2023;

(f) the wife asked Mr R to sell his shares to her for $170,000, but Mr R did not agree;

(g) the wife lodged a caveat over the title to the property to stop the sale process;

(h) prior to settlement, on 30 September 2024 Mr R and the wife agreed that the wife would purchase his shares for $210,000 using Self-Managed Super Fund 1;

(i) Mr R then intended to use the proceeds to pay back the loan from the husband’s present wife;

(j) at some point Mr R wrote to the husband asking for him to pay him the money alleged to be due from the wife for his shares;

(k) the husband at that stage still controlled Self-Managed Super Fund 1 and so caused the trustee of that fund to pay Mr R $210,000;

(l) as matters turned out, the wife denied any agreement with Mr R to purchase his shares and she demanded that the husband make good the amount that he had taken from Self-Managed Super Fund 1 and given to Mr R;

(m) the husband agreed to pay back Self-Managed Super Fund 1 from “the $600,000 cash allocated to me and hold in [AA Trust] account”; and

(n) the husband would deal with Mr R after settlement.

The husband says that although he agreed to return the $210,000 and deal with Mr R after settlement, the amount of $210,000 should be reimbursed to him because it has been paid out of a sum of $600,000 to which he was otherwise entitled.

The first difficulty associated with this claim is that is it unsupported by any evidence. Whilst it is true that the husband sought to rely upon evidence from Mr R, because he was not available for cross-examination, I refused to permit him to read Mr R’s affidavit.

The husband also relies upon annexure MRM27 to support his claim. That document is a handwritten record of what the husband says is an agreement in principle reached by him with the wife. Whilst the document records a cash payment to the husband of $600,000, it says nothing about the source of those funds, or what part of the parties’ asset pool it represents. According to the husband’s evidence, the $600,00 was part of the proceeds of sale of Property 5 at M Street in the wife’s solicitors’ trust account. He says that the $600,000 would be allocated and paid to him. However, he says that there were deductions made so that he did not receive the whole $600,000. I have set those out earlier. Annexure MRM27 does not support the husband’s submission that the $210,000 is part of the $600,000 that both parties agreed to allocate and transfer to the husband.

Of this claim, the wife says that during the proceeding and prior to the consent orders being made, the husband has asserted that she had agreed to purchase Mr R’s shares in E Pty Ltd for $210,000. The wife denies the allegation. He knew of her denials but nonetheless transferred to Mr R $210,000 from Self-Managed Super Fund 1 without her consent.

There is no share sale agreement in evidence. There is no transfer of the shares in evidence. The latter would be expected if the sale price has been paid to Mr R as the husband alleges.

The wife also points out that Mr R is involved in a separate dispute against E Pty Ltd concerning an alleged loan to the company “many years ago”. Moreover, she emphasises that the consent orders do not require her to transfer any funds to Mr R or to the husband on behalf of Mr R in respect of any claim or liability.

This claim made by the husband must be dismissed. It is not an order that could or should be made by way of enforcement of the consent orders. Nor is it an order that might be made concerning the implementation or interpretation of the consent orders or any one of them. It is a claim that stands separately to the consent orders and depends upon the resolution of basic factual questions concerning the agreement, if any, between the wife and Mr R.

Order 4 of the Application – Enforcement

By order 4 in his Application – Enforcement, the husband seeks that the wife “pay the interest of the NAB loan ($1,730,495 @ 7.699% = $11,102.57 per month) from March 2025 to the month when the sale proceeds are distributed because she hold up the sale proceeds”. This “NAB loan” is a reference to the loan secured over the O Street properties and perhaps a property site controlled by the husband.

The evidence shows that by August 2025 the husband was able to offer the wife clear title to the O Street properties. The consent orders did not specify how he was to do that, and as his own evidence shows, he intended to borrow further funds that would enable him to clear the relevant titles ready for transfer to the wife. It is by no means clear on the evidence that the interest liability for which the husband now wants compensation, would not, in any event, have been incurred by him.

The husband submits that the wife ought to pay the interest “because she [held] up the sale proceeds”. This ground must fail because the sale of the O Street properties was held up by the husband’s inability to discharge the loan or provide a sufficient replacement security to release it. Once he was in a position to transfer the relevant titles, a dispute developed about the disbursement of the proceeds from the M Street Property. The deductions that the husband wishes to make from those proceeds, to his benefit, are without foundation. He is just as much to blame as the wife for the delay in distribution of the relevant funds.

There is no basis in the facts or in law for the making of any order as the husband seeks in respect of this claim.

Order 6 of the Application – Enforcement

The husband seeks that the wife pay $76,362 representing the husband’s legal and tax advice and application costs incidental to this application. However, whilst both parties have adopted unreasonable positions, much of the issues relating to the performance of these orders lies with the husband, there is no basis that he should have his costs of this application, including and legal or accounting costs.

This claim is rejected.

Order 7 of the Application – Enforcement

The husband seeks that the wife “compensate [K2 Pty Ltd] for the interest loss for GST ($37,610.14) of sale of [M Street] [Property 2] and [Property 5] at ATO default interest rate (11.36% per year) or $356 per month from 15 September 2025 until the fund is transferred to [K2 Pty Ltd]. This interest should be deducted from [Ms Michelaki’s] entitlement to the sale proceeds referred to in paragraph 13(q)(7) of the final order and transferred to [K2 Pty Ltd]”.

This claim assumes that the GST payable by K2 Pty Ltd ought to be paid out of the proceeds of the M Street Property pursuant to order 13(q)(1). But for the reasons I have given above, that claim does not succeed. The GST liability is the company’s alone to bear. This claim is dismissed.

Order 8 of the Application – Enforcement

The husband seeks that the wife “compensate [K2 Pty Ltd] for the interest loss of Capital Gain Tax ($856,605.6) for the sale of the [M Street Property] ([Property 1,2,3,4 and 5]) from the due date to the date when the fund is released and paid to ATO at ATO default interest rate (11.36% per year), which is $8,109.2 per month”. He seeks that this compensation be paid by deduction from the wife’s share of the M Street Property proceeds.

Leaving aside for one moment the inability of the court to make this order on the application before it (because it is neither an enforcement order nor within order 78 of the consent orders), the evidence demonstrates that the relevant tax is not due for payment until 15 May 2026 (see annexure MSM-19 to the wife’s affidavit and annexure MRM32 to the husband’s affidavit).

The time for payment has not passed and no interest has accrued on the tax amount. It is not outstanding. The payment will be able to be made promptly upon delivery of my reasons and orders quelling the parties’ disputes.

This claim is dismissed.

Order 9 of the Application – Enforcement

The husband seeks that the wife “compensate [K2 Pty Ltd] for the interest loss of Capital Gain Tax ($93,459.60) for the sale of [Property 5 at M Street] from 10/06/2025 to March 2026”. For the reasons I have given above, the tax payable on the relevant capital gain falls as a liability to be borne by the company alone. Thus, even if the court was minded to entertain the order sought by the husband, the claim could not succeed.

Moreover, the evidence shows that the relevant tax was paid on 12 June 2025. A general interest charge of $804.39 was raised for the period 1 July 2024 to 12 June 2025 but was then reversed on 1 July 2025. No interest, in fact, has been levied in respect of the overdue payment of the relevant tax which has now been paid in full.

This claim is dismissed.

Order 10 of the Application – Enforcement

The husband seeks that the wife “should pay the interest for the money [Mr R] borrow from me. The interest is 15% per year from 25th October 2024 to March 2026 (18 months) or $47,250. This interest should be deducted from Jenny’s entitlement to the sale proceeds and paid to my account”.

This claim fails because:

(a) Mr R did not borrow any money from the husband on the evidence;

(b) on the husband’s case, Mr R borrowed money from the husband’s wife;

(c) it is not an order that can be made by way of enforcement;

(d) it is not an order that is within order 78 of the consent orders; and

(e) there is simply no evidence sufficient to support any findings that might lead to the relief sought by the husband.

Order 3 of the wife’s further amended Application in a Proceeding

I turn now to the relief claimed in the wife’s further amended Application in a Proceeding.

The wife seeks that within 7 days, the husband pay to a bank account of the self-managed superannuation fund known as “[Self-Managed Super Fund 1]” nominated by the wife in writing the sum of $18,000 plus interest calculated in accordance with the FCCR.

Self-Managed Super Fund 1 is administered by a corporate trustee, V Pty Ltd of which the parties were the directors at the time of the consent orders. The parties were members of the fund.

It is uncontroversial that after the consent orders were made, the husband caused $6,000 to be transferred to him on three different occasions namely, 16 April 2025, 5 May 2025 and 6 June 2025.

However, pursuant to order 66 of the consent orders, “[p]ending the transfer of the husband’s transferable benefits, each party is restrained from dealing with, charging, encumbering or disposing of any of the assets of [Self-Managed Super Fund 1] other than in accordance with the terms of this Order”.

The husband asserts that the three withdrawals were payments to him in respect of a valid management fee incurred by Self-Managed Super Fund 1. He claims that he assisted Self-Managed Super Fund 1 and reasons that these payments were necessary because the wife “did not perform her duty”. The wife disputes this denying that the husband has done any material work for the administration of Self-Managed Super Fund 1. She points out that there was no resolution nor agreement with her authorising the payments. Moreover, the husband was restrained from dealing or otherwise disposing of the assets of the Fund whilst the splitting payment was pending.

I accept the wife’s submissions. There was no basis for the husband to charge the fund management fees as he did. His decision to do so in the circumstances described by him, unsupported by the agreement of the wife, does not authorise his actions. The withdrawals were in direct breach of order 66 of the consent orders. The absence of any authority, or reasonable basis, for effecting the transfers is reflected in the manner in which the wife decided how much he would charge the fund for his purported management fees described by him in his affidavit filed 28 February 2026, at [87] in this way:

After a few email communications with [Ms Michelaki’s] solicitor, I still did not receive the transfer documents of [Self-Managed Super Fund 1]. I had to charge management fee to get thing moving.

I charged $600 per hour for 10 hours per month. As a senior project manager, $600 per hour is the lowest in the market

The wife seeks the restoration of the payments to Self-Managed Super Fund 1. Ample power exists to make orders to effect this claim either as a consequential order for the purposes of order 78 of the consent orders, or as an order within the powers set out in FCCR 11.07(f) to prevent the dissipation or wastage of property. It is appropriate for there to be an order that the husband restore to Self-Managed Super Fund 1 the sum of $18,000 plus interest from 19 June 2025 to the date of the restoration at the rates fixed from time to time by the FCCR. The amount should be deducted from the husband’s share of the M Street Property proceeds.

The wife seeks an order for interest on the funds wrongly taken by the husband. An order for interest is appropriate. The amount claimed and the way in which it is calculated are both set out in Exhibit 2. The amount claimed of $1,071.55 is appropriate.

Order 4 of the wife’s further amended Application in a Proceeding

The order sought by the wife, in effect, directs her solicitors to disburse their funds in their trust account by paying $856,605.60 to the ATO on account of K2 Pty Ltd’s income tax and then the balance to the parties 65% to the wife and 35% to the husband. Further, she seeks an order that:

(c) for the purpose of calculating the applicant husband’s entitlement to 35% of the sale proceeds pursuant to subparagraph 13(q)(7) of the final orders dated 20 December 2024, such amount is taken to include the sum of $60,834 already paid to the Australian Tax Office at his direction from the gross sale proceeds on account of [K2 Pty Ltd’s] GST liability for the financial quarter ending 31 March 2025.

Thus, two issues are revealed by the wife’s orders. I have dealt with the first concerning where she is to pay the amount for K2 Pty Ltd’s tax earlier in these reasons. That amount should be paid to the husband or at his direction.

The second issue concerns an amount of $60,834 said to have already been paid on the husband’s behalf from the M Street Property sale proceeds directly to the Australia Tax Office to pay outstanding GST owed by K2 Pty Ltd is a distribution the husband for the purposes of order 13(q)(7). As to that issue, the facts are:

(a) on 5 June 2025 the husband sent to the wife’s solicitors a business activity statement for the financial quarter ending 31 March 2025 for K2 Pty Ltd showing GST payable in the amount of $60,834 with a request that the wife agree to release the $60,834 to K2 Pty Ltd;

(b) on 28 June 2025, the parties authorised the solicitors who held the M Street Property sale proceeds on trust at the time, to pay the $60,834 directly to the Australian Tax Office as requested by the husband; and

(c) the relevant amount was goods and services tax arising from the sales of Property 1, Property 3 and Property 4.

The wife contends that this payment is outside of the scope of any of the deductions in order 13(q) of the consent orders. She does not accept that the orders make provision for the payment of K2 Pty Ltd’s GST from the sale proceeds.

She claims that because the payment was made for the husband’s benefit, it ought to be accounted for as part of the calculation of his ultimate 35% share of the net sale proceeds.

I have already rejected the husband’s claim that $37,610.14 should be deducted from the net sale proceeds from the M Street Property for K2 Pty Ltd’s GST liability arising from the sale of Property 2 and Property 5. For the same reasons, this liability for GST should be borne by K2 Pty Ltd alone.

The payment of that amount at the husband’s direction and with the wife’s agreement was not an agreement by her that the amount was a legitimate deduction from the nett proceeds of sale, but was rather, an early distribution to the husband of his ultimate entitlements under the orders.

To reflect that reality, the amount that was paid needs to be taken up in the final distribution of proceeds pursuant to order 13(q)(7) of the consent orders.

Orders 5 and 6 of the wife’s further amended Application in a Proceeding

The wife seeks the following orders:

In the event the applicant husband elects to account for the respondent wife’s 65% entitlement to the [M Street Property] sale proceeds by way of a dividend paid by [K2 Pty Ltd] to the [Michelaki Family Trust] and subsequently a distribution to the respondent wife as a beneficiary of that trust, then the applicant husband is restrained from distributing any income to the wife other than that specific dividend income.

In the event the applicant husband causes [K2 Pty Ltd] to lodge any amended tax return within 2 years of the date of this Order and an amended assessment is issued by the Australian Tax Office resulting in the company receiving a refund with respect to the $856,605.60 paid pursuant to subparagraph 4(a) of this Order, then:

(a) the applicant husband will notify the wife of the amended assessment within 7 days of issue; and

(b) the applicant husband will pay the wife’s nominated bank account a sum equivalent to 65% of the refunded amount within 21 days.

The wife made no submissions in support of these orders. I decline to make them. They work a change to the substance of the orders, not merely the mechanics of their execution. They are orders that were available to be included in the consent orders when those terms were agreed and settled between the parties. These claims are refused.

Order 7 of the wife’s further amended Application in a Proceeding

The wife seeks an order that a registrar of this court execute certain documents on the husband’s behalf pursuant to s 106A of the Act.

This dispute concerns an ASIC Form 484 prepared by the wife to effect the husband’s resignation as a director of E Pty Ltd ACN [...], a minute of meeting prepared by the respondent wife acknowledging the husband’s resignation, and the wife’s appointment, as director of E Pty Ltd ACN [...]. The husband has refused to sign the documents because he has not been paid, what he claims he is entitled to charge the wife as a management fee. There is no basis to his claim however. His obligation under the order to sign documents necessary to effect the relevant transfers is not subject to the discharge of any such asserted liability.

However, the order is unnecessary. The consent orders already contain a provision to identical effect in order 68. If the documents are presented to a registrar for signature, they should be executed in accordance with order 68.

Costs

Both parties seek the costs of this application, but neither should have an order for costs. Both have adopted positions in respect of various issues that were plainly unreasonable and against the terms of the orders.

Disposition

I make the orders set out at the commencement of these reasons.
